name: Process-to-Activation Method
intro: A business-first method that starts with operational understanding and ends with a system people actually use. Five stages, each with an input, a question set and an output that the next stage depends on.

stages.name: Discover
stages.purpose: Understand the business before discussing the system.
activities: Separating the urgent problem from the eventual ambition
questionsAsked: How does this business actually make money?
Who does this work today, and who is accountable when it goes wrong?
What triggered this request now, rather than last year?
What happens today when the process fails?
Which of these requirements would you drop if you had to launch in six weeks?
What data exists, where does it live, and who trusts it?
risksChecked: The stated problem is not the real problem
One stakeholder is describing a process the rest do not follow
The request assumes an application before the process is known
Data is not ready and no one has said so
clientResponsibilities: Access to the people who run the process, not only the people who commissioned the project

stages.name: Map
stages.purpose: Document what is really happening, including the parts nobody puts in the process document.
questionsAsked: Show me the last five real records — not the example.
Where does this step wait, and what is it waiting for?
What do you do when the customer is an exception?
Which spreadsheet is the real system?
Who re-types this data, and into what?
What report do you build by hand every month?
risksChecked: The documented process and the real process differ
Exceptions are the majority, not the minority
A handoff has no owner
Reporting depends on a person, not a system
clientResponsibilities: Time from operational staff, not only management

stages.name: Redesign
stages.purpose: Design a simpler future state — and decide what does not make Phase 1.
activities: Removing steps that exist only to compensate for other steps
Introducing automation only where it removes real effort
questionsAsked: What is this step protecting against?
If this approval disappeared, what would break?
What is the minimum state model that still tells the truth?
Which improvement would the team feel first?
What must be true for this to work on day one?
risksChecked: The redesign optimises for management visibility at the cost of the person doing the work
Automation hides an accountability gap instead of closing it
Phase 1 is too large to land
The new process assumes data the business does not have

stages.name: Architect
stages.purpose: Convert the future process into a practical system design that can be estimated.
inputs: Data sources and systems of record
questionsAsked: Which system owns this record, and which system only references it?
Is this standard functionality, configuration, custom work or someone else's product?
What happens to this integration when the other side is down?
Can a non-technical user maintain this in a year?
What is the simpler version of this design?
What does this cost to build, and what does it cost to keep?
risksChecked: Two systems claim the same source of truth
Custom work has been chosen for a preference, not a requirement
An integration depends on a client credential nobody has requested
The permission model does not survive the org chart
Scope has expanded quietly since Redesign
clientResponsibilities: Confirming role structure and data ownership

stages.name: Activate
stages.purpose: Move from a design to a system in use.
questionsAsked: Does this build match the requirement, or only the demo?
Which scenario has nobody tested because it is inconvenient?
Who is the first person to use this on go-live morning, and do they know?
What is a defect, and what is a change request?
What does 'done' mean for this phase, in writing?
risksChecked: UAT is being run by people who wrote the requirement rather than people who will use the system
Change requests are being absorbed as defects
Training is scheduled after go-live
Data migration is treated as a task rather than a project
Adoption has no owner

decisionFramework.name: The Eleven Questions
decisionFramework.intro: Every requirement goes through the same set of questions before it becomes a build. It is not a formality — most of the scope arguments a project will ever have are decided here, quietly, or later, expensively.

tests.question: Is it standard functionality or custom work?
tests.whyItMatters: Custom work carries a maintenance cost forever. It has to be worth it, not merely possible.

tests.question: Is it in scope, or out?
tests.whyItMatters: A requirement with no boundary is a requirement with no estimate.

tests.question: What are the dependencies?
tests.whyItMatters: Most delays are not build time. They are waiting for a credential, a decision or another team.

tests.question: What must the client provide?
tests.whyItMatters: Unstated client responsibilities become the delivery team's problem at the worst possible moment.

tests.question: What are the risks?
tests.whyItMatters: A risk named in week one is a plan. Named in week ten, it is an escalation.

tests.question: Is it suitable for non-technical users?
tests.whyItMatters: A system only technical people can operate has not solved the business problem; it has moved it.

tests.question: Is it implementation-ready?
tests.whyItMatters: If a developer would have to guess, the requirement is not finished.

tests.question: Is there a simpler approach?
tests.whyItMatters: The elegant design and the maintainable design are often not the same design.

tests.question: What is the business effect?
tests.whyItMatters: If no one can state the effect, the requirement is a preference.

tests.question: How will it be tested?
tests.whyItMatters: A requirement that cannot be tested cannot be accepted.

tests.question: What are the acceptance criteria?
tests.whyItMatters: 'Done' has to be written down before the build, or it will be negotiated after it.

testingModel.intro: Quality is layered. Each layer catches what the previous one is structurally blind to.

layers.name: Client UAT
checks: Real scenarios from their own operation
Issues classified as defect or change request
